pathophysiology_summary: Molluscum contagiosum is a benign self-limiting poxvirus infection producing clusters of firm, pearly/flesh-coloured, dome-shaped umbilicated papules, spread by contact and autoinoculation (linear lesions along scratch lines). After months, a brisk host immune response often makes lesions acutely red, swollen and tender — the 'BOTE' (Beginning Of The End) sign — heralding viral clearance. This mimics bacterial cellulitis/abscess, but the presence of central umbilicated papules at the centre of the erythema, localisation strictly around the lesions, absence of honey-crusting/true purulence/fluctuance/lymphangitis, and an afebrile well child point to inflammatory molluscum rather than infection. Recognising BOTE avoids unnecessary systemic antibiotics; management is reassurance, a mild topical corticosteroid to the surrounding itchy eczematous skin, a barrier cream to reduce friction, simple analgesia, short nails and hand hygiene (to limit autoinoculation), and NOT squeezing the lesions. The must-not-miss is a true secondary bacterial cellulitis/abscess (spreading hot/hard redness, fever, fluctuant pus), which is screened negative here. Discriminators: genital warts are hyperkeratotic/cauliflower-like (not umbilicated), and varicella is an acute widespread blistering rash with fever, not a chronic localised cluster.

differential.evidence_basis: Chronic umbilicated papules now acutely inflamed — classic BOTE.
Acute groin redness/pain must be screened for true infection (fever, spreading hot/hard redness, pus, fluctuance).
Hyperkeratotic cauliflower-like, not smooth umbilicated papules.
Acute widespread blistering with fever, not a chronic localised cluster.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Recognise BOTE (avoid antibiotics), screen for true bacterial infection, and reassure/treat itch and friction.

differential.evidence_basis: Umbilicated papules centrally, localised erythema, afebrile — inflammatory clearance, not infection.
No fever, spreading hot/hard erythema, fluctuance or pus — remains a safety-net.
Working diagnosis: reassurance, mild topical steroid to surrounding eczema, barrier/friction reduction, hygiene; bacterial-infection safety-net.

red_flags.description: True secondary bacterial cellulitis/abscess (spreading hot/hard redness down the leg, fever, fluctuant pus, lymphangitis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would need antibiotics ± drainage; distinguished from the localised BOTE reaction.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Giving systemic antibiotics/ED referral for the benign BOTE inflammatory reaction.
Advising squeezing/curetting, spreading the virus and causing scarring.
Not screening for genuine secondary bacterial infection.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Reassure (BOTE = clearing), treat surrounding itch with a mild topical steroid, barrier cream and analgesia, short nails and hand hygiene; review/ED only if true cellulitis/abscess features develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Over-treating BOTE as cellulitis, or missing a genuine secondary infection.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Molluscum contagiosum is self-limiting; inflammation (BOTE) signals resolution and does not need antibiotics; manage itch and friction; treat only genuine secondary bacterial infection.
